mnemonics.title: CPU COMPONENTS
mnemonics.content: CU ALU REGISTERS - CONTROL UNIT ALU REGISTERS WORKING: CU CONTROLS, ALU CALCULATES, REGISTERS STORE TEMPORARILY. REMEMBER: C U A R - CONTROL UNIT ARITHMETIC REGISTERS.

mnemonics.title: INSTRUCTION CYCLE
mnemonics.content: FETCH DECODE EXECUTE STORE - FIRST THE INSTRUCTION IS FETCHED (GOT), THEN DECODED (READ), THEN EXECUTED (DONE), THEN STORED (SAVED). Mnemonic: FRED SAYS EVERYONE DEVELOPS SKILLS.

mnemonics.title: SYSTEM BUS TYPES
mnemonics.content: DATA ADDRESS CONTROL - DATA CARRIES INFORMATION, ADDRESS SAYS WHERE TO GO, CONTROL SAYS WHAT TO DO. Mnemonic: DAD (Data/Address/Control) - DAD TELLS US WHAT TO DO.

mnemonics.title: MEMORY TYPES
mnemonics.content: ROM RAM - ROM RETAINS, RAM RENEWS. ROM keeps data when power is off (Read Only), RAM refreshes constantly for active use.

mnemonics.title: MEMORY HIERARCHY
mnemonics.content: REGISTERS CACHE RAM DISK - Registers are FASTEST but SMALLEST, Cache is FAST, RAM is MEDIUM, Disk is SLOW but LARGEST. Mnemonic: R-C-R-D: REgisters Come Before RAM During execution.

acronyms.usage: The brain of the computer that executes instructions
Performs mathematical and logical operations in CPU
Directs operation of processor and controls data flow
Primary volatile memory for temporary data storage
Allows I/O devices to transfer data without CPU intervention
Devices for data entry and output display
Firmware that initializes hardware during boot process
Signal line used by devices to request CPU attention
Standard bus for connecting hardware devices

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box representing CPU. Inside, show three boxes: Control Unit (top), ALU (middle), Registers (bottom). Arrows show data flow between them. External arrows connect to Memory and I/O through buses.
Create circular flow: FETCH arrow to memory → DECODE arrow → EXECUTE arrow (to ALU) → STORE arrow back to memory. Show PC (Program Counter) pointing to next instruction.

Draw an inverted pyramid: Top (smallest, fastest) = Registers, Next level = Cache, Next = RAM (Main Memory), Bottom (largest, slowest) = Secondary Storage (HDD/SSD). Show cost decreasing and access time increasing as you go down.
Draw horizontal lines: Top line = Data Bus (bidirectional arrow), Middle = Address Bus (one-way from CPU), Bottom = Control Bus (various signals). Show CPU on left, Memory and I/O on right.
Show CPU executing main program. When interrupt occurs, draw curved arrow to 'Interrupt Handler' box, then return arrow to main program with 'Resume' label.

analogies.analogy: CPU is like the BRAIN of the computer - it thinks, calculates, and controls everything, just as your brain processes information and makes decisions.
RAM is like a DESK - it holds items you're currently working on. When you turn off the computer, the desk is cleared, just like RAM loses its contents.
Hard disk is like a FILING CABINET - it stores everything permanently until you delete it, even when power is off.
System bus is like the ROAD SYSTEM in a city - data travels on these roads between the brain (CPU), workspace (RAM), and external areas (I/O devices).
Cache is like a NOTEPAD next to your desk - faster to access than the main desk (RAM), holding frequently used items within arm's reach.
Control Unit is like a PROJECT MANAGER - it gives orders, coordinates tasks, and ensures everything happens in the right sequence.
ALU is like a CALCULATOR - it performs all the mathematical calculations and logical comparisons needed to complete tasks.
Interrupt is like a PHONE RINGING while you're working - it pauses your current task, you answer the call (handle the interrupt), then return to work.
